verdict: July 9, 2026 3:33 PM PDT — The sweep is directionally sound on the implementation claims: Simple defaults, non-host lock, multiplier removal, endpoint list, DATA_DIR persistence wording, and the operator-decryptable disclosure all match the code. The operator-decryptable wording is an accurate clarification, not a new technical commitment, and the July 9 effective-date bumps are defensible on the pages with substantive content changes. I would not call the shape fully sound yet because it leaves a stale README visual, stale public-page copy, and split backlog references.

findings.title: README still embeds the old per-person-only architecture diagram
findings.claim: The textual ASCII diagram now shows `selectActiveTotals()` branching to Simple vs per-person, but the README still embeds `dev-docs/meeting-cost-architecture.png`, whose source says the presenter sets each person's hourly opportunity cost and does not show the Simple default path. That leaves the most visible architecture diagram contradicting the new default model and the AC requiring the architecture diagram to reflect cost-model selection.
findings.alternative: Regenerate or replace the SVG/PNG so the rendered README visual matches the new ASCII shape: cost model -> Simple attendees x average rate or host-only per-person table -> aggregate overlay. If maintaining two diagrams is not worth it, remove the PNG from README and keep the ASCII as the single diagram.
findings.win: Removes a public stale claim and prevents future readers from treating the old per-person-only flow as the architecture despite the code using `selectActiveTotals()`.

findings.title: Public policy/support copy still describes the old per-person-only data model
findings.claim: The public docs still say the presenter estimates each person's opportunity cost and can set a default, and Support still asks what number to enter for each person. That misses the shipped Simple-default model, where the default input is average rate plus attendee count and per-person values are host/co-host-only. The Support storage FAQ also still says only the user can retrieve settings, which undercuts the new operator-decryptable disclosure even if the intended meaning is product-level retrieval.
findings.alternative: Sweep the public pages that explain use/data entry with neutral wording: default Simple stores average-rate/count settings, hosts/co-hosts may optionally store per-person rules, participants never see either, and data is account-scoped but not end-to-end encrypted. Replace the Support FAQ's 'only you can retrieve' wording with a short pointer to the Privacy Policy's operator-decryptable explanation.
findings.win: Makes the legal/support surface consistent with both the current UI and the new disclosure, avoiding a remaining public contradiction after the sweep.

findings.title: Deferred follow-ups are recorded in a different backlog than the roadmap points to
findings.claim: The story adds BUG-1 and OPS-1 to root `BACKLOG.md`, but `dev-docs/roadmap.md` still states tactical per-feature work lives in `reviews/backlog.md` and its execution plan inventories open work from that file. The new follow-ups are therefore durable in one file but invisible to the roadmap's advertised work source, leaving split-brain planning docs.
findings.alternative: Pick one canonical backlog for this repo. Either update the roadmap references/execution-plan wording to point at root `BACKLOG.md`, or also add/link these two items in `reviews/backlog.md` if that remains the tactical detail store.
findings.win: Keeps deferred work discoverable from the roadmap and removes a second source-of-truth failure in the docs-only sweep.
